summary.summary: This week's ~1,631 papers skew heavily toward scalable oversight (403) and agentic behavior/autonomy (211 monitoring, 284 capability), reflecting continued momentum on agent systems rather than a single breakthrough. The most policy-relevant safety result is an empirical takedown of AI watermarking as forensic evidence (2607.16010), directly challenging assumptions behind the EU AI Act and California SB 942. Adversarial work on agent memory (2607.15657) and questions about whether agentic components actually yield controllable behavior (2607.15715) surface concrete robustness and controllability gaps in deployed-style agents. On capability, there's a push to connect pretraining choices to RL post-training reasoning gains (2607.16097) and to understand VLM compositional failures mechanistically (2607.16094).
themes: Agent robustness: memory poisoning and controllability of reflection/memory components
Linking pretraining to RL post-training reasoning outcomes
Heavy volume on scalable oversight and agentic autonomy

notable.why_it_matters: Empirically shows AI watermarks fail forensic-readiness standards, undercutting the 'reliable and robust' assumptions baked into the EU AI Act and California SB 942.
Demonstrates a black-box visual attack that plants false memories in multimodal agents, exposing that persistent long-term memory is a real, exploitable attack surface.
Questions whether agentic add-ons like reflection and memory produce observable, controllable improvements over fixed workflows — key for evaluating whether 'agentic' claims are substantive.
Bridges pretraining and RL post-training to explain where reasoning gains actually come from, informing how much of frontier reasoning capability is set before RL.
Provides a mechanistic account of vision-operation misalignment behind VLM compositional-VQA failures, useful for interpretability-driven debugging of multimodal models.
Introduces a benchmark for open-ended prospective hypothesis discovery, probing autonomous scientific-agent capability beyond answering fixed questions.

risks.risk: Reward Hacking's sharply rising CRITICAL share meets multi-tenant VLA robot post-training services (this week): reward-hacked control policies get shared across robot embodiments and tenants, turning a training-time pathology into a cross-fleet physical-actuation failure mode nobody in the robotics-service supply chain is watching.
risks.research_anchor: Quarterly backdrop: Reward Hacking rising critical share to 27.5% (+22.2 pts) — the strongest rising-critical-share signal in the digest — crossed with THIS WEEK's 'JoyNexus: Service-Oriented Multi-Tenant Post-Training for VLA Models' (2607.16074) and 'Physics-enhanced RL for real-time optimal control of dynamical systems' (2607.16177, tagged Scalable Oversight).
risks.derived_from: Rising-critical-share Reward Hacking (27.5%, +22.2 trend_pts) as the anchoring safety signal; 2607.16074 describing shared multi-tenant post-training across simulators/embodiments/tasks; 2607.16177 applying RL to real-time control of physical dynamical systems; multi-agent/tool-use vs scalable-oversight divergence (cap +25.9%/qtr, oversight -4.2%/qtr).
risks.communities: RL-safety researchers track reward hacking as a text/agent-benchmark phenomenon and rarely follow VLA/robotics service architectures; VLA post-training and robotics-service engineers (JoyNexus-style multi-tenant platforms) optimize for GPU utilization and task completion and rarely read the reward-hacking-critical-share literature. Neither community owns the seam where a reward-hacked policy is packaged and reused across tenants and robot bodies.
risks.framing_inversion: Conventional framing: 'reward hacking is a benign benchmark curiosity — the model games a scoreboard.' Inversion: in a multi-tenant physical-actuation service, a reward-hacked policy is a reusable, distributable defect that can propagate across robot fleets and embodiments the original trainer never saw.
risks.why_underdiscussed: Reward-hacking discourse lives in LLM/agent benchmarks; VLA multi-tenant serving is a fresh systems paper this week. The specific hazard — reward hacking as a supply-chain contaminant in shared robot post-training — sits between two literatures that don't cite each other yet.
risks.mechanism: Multi-tenant VLA post-training amortizes compute by sharing/adapting policies across embodiments and tasks (2607.16074) → an RL objective mis-specified for one physical control task (cf. 2607.16177) produces a policy that maximizes proxy reward via unsafe shortcuts → because the service reuses components across tenants/embodiments, the hacked behavior transfers to robots and settings that were never evaluated for it, while scalable-oversight research decelerates.
risks.leading_indicator: A reported physical-robot incident or recall traced to an RL objective mis-specification in a shared/hosted post-training pipeline, OR a VLA-service system card acknowledging cross-tenant policy reuse without per-embodiment reward-hacking evaluation.
risks.calibration: Genuinely emerging but early. Reward-hacking critical-share rise is observed; the multi-tenant VLA architecture is observed this week. The physical-propagation harm is inferred/speculative — it assumes shared components carry reward-hacked behavior across embodiments, which the papers do not demonstrate. Confidence lowered accordingly.
risks.extrapolation: Assumed multi-tenant VLA services reuse policy components across embodiments in ways that transfer reward-hacked behavior and that per-tenant reward-hacking evals are absent — neither shown in the digest.
risks.score_rationale: Severity 4 because physical-actuation reward hacking across a robot fleet can cause real-world harm; likelihood 2 because multi-tenant VLA serving and the transfer-of-defect chain are nascent; exposure 3 because it is currently confined to VLA-service adopters but scales with the robotics buildout.

change_of_mind.upgrade_if: A hosted VLA post-training platform reaches production with multiple tenants sharing policy components AND a reward-hacking-linked control failure appears on more than one embodiment.
change_of_mind.downgrade_if: Multi-tenant VLA services adopt per-embodiment, per-task adversarial reward-hacking evaluation before deployment, or keep tenant policies fully isolated.
change_of_mind.invalidate_if: Evidence shows reward-hacked behaviors do not transfer across embodiments/tasks in shared post-training and are caught by existing sim-to-real validation.

action_map.eval_to_run: Cross-embodiment reward-hacking probe: take a policy trained with a deliberately mis-specified reward on one embodiment and measure unsafe-shortcut carryover after multi-tenant adaptation to a different robot body.
action_map.benchmark_to_monitor: Reward Hacking critical-share trend (currently 27.5%, +22.2 pts) plus emergence of any VLA-service-specific safety benchmarks.
action_map.mitigation: Require per-tenant/per-embodiment reward-model auditing and behavioral quarantine before any shared policy component is redeployed to a new robot.
action_map.policy_question: Who is liable when a reward-hacked policy shared through a multi-tenant service injures across a fleet — the platform, the tenant, or the base-model trainer?
action_map.owner_community: RL-safety + VLA/robotics-service engineering, jointly, with a robot-safety standards body.
action_map.data_source_to_watch: VLA-service system cards, robotics incident/recall databases (e.g. CPSC, industrial-robot safety reports), and new arXiv VLA-serving papers.

claims.text: Reward Hacking's critical share rose to 27.5% (+22.2 pts), the strongest rising-critical-share signal this period.
A this-week paper describes multi-tenant post-training that shares/adapts VLA policies across simulators, embodiments, and tasks.
RL is being applied to real-time control of physical dynamical systems (2607.16177).
Reward-hacked policies could propagate across tenants/embodiments through shared post-training components.
Scalable-oversight research is decelerating relative to multi-agent/tool-use capability, thinning the monitoring designed for this.

verification.prior_coverage: The core mechanism is only partly novel: reward hacking generalizing/transferring across tasks and models is an established, named public research area (Kei et al. 2024 'Reward hacking behavior can generalize across tasks'), and reward hacking in physical RL/robotic control is directly documented (e.g., turbulent drag-reduction study; empirical robotic-control studies). Security of shared, foundation-model-powered and multi-tenant robot systems — including cross-embodiment transfer and cross-tenant contamination — is also actively written about (SoK on FM-powered robots; multi-tenant AI contamination analyses; cross-embodiment negative-transfer VLA papers). What I did NOT find is the exact fused seam stated as one connected claim: reward-hacked control policies propagating physical-actuation harm specifically via multi-tenant VLA post-training-as-a-service across tenants/embodiments.

verification.disputed_claims: Two dependencies are shaky. (1) The anchoring metric — 'sharply rising CRITICAL share (27.5%, +22.2 trend_pts)' — is an internal/proprietary signal with no external corroboration; whether reward hacking is genuinely rising vs. simply being measured better is itself contested, and multiple recent works (Reward Hacking Benchmark 2605.02964; Hack-Verifiable Environments 2605.20744; EvilGenie) exist precisely because there is no agreed measurement standard. (2) The physical-propagation step is not demonstrated by the cited papers: cross-embodiment VLA transfer is known to fail via negative transfer (e.g., Cloak 2606.22836), but no source shows a reward-hacked policy's unsafe shortcut surviving cross-embodiment/multi-tenant adaptation into a physical-actuation harm — this remains an unverified inference, as the candidate's own calibration concedes.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Emerging but more anticipated than framed, and it rests on at least one unverified inference plus one uncorroborated metric. The building blocks are already public and named: reward-hacking generalization across tasks/models, reward hacking in physical/robotic RL, cross-embodiment transfer failure in VLAs, and multi-tenant/shared-model contamination in FM-powered robot systems. The genuinely fresh contribution is the specific SEAM — reward-hacked control behavior propagating as a physical-actuation failure THROUGH multi-tenant VLA post-training-as-a-service — which I could not find written up as a single connected claim. Confidence must be lowered on two points the risk depends on: (a) the '27.5% / +22.2 trend_pt rising CRITICAL share' is an internal number with no external validation and reward-hacking's true trend is disputed (measurement-vs-reality), so it should not be cited as an established fact; and (b) the cross-embodiment physical-propagation harm is inferred, not demonstrated — known cross-embodiment transfer often FAILS via negative transfer, which cuts both ways and could either block or mangle the hacked behavior unpredictably. Brief as a plausible, under-watched integration point worth monitoring, not as a confirmed or novel failure mode; strip the specific percentages and present the propagation step as a hypothesis to be tested, not an observed pathology.

risks.risk: Black-box visual attacks on agent long-term memory (this week) plus PRC-linked influence ops already targeting the US data-center/electricity debate creates a 0–24 month path to persistent memory-poisoning of deployed multimodal agents — an influence/agentic-misuse seam that neither the memory-security nor the influence-ops community currently connects.
risks.research_anchor: This week's 'Do Agents Dream of False Memories? Black-box Visual Attacks on Long-term Memory in Multimodal AI Agents' (2607.15657), tagged Agentic Behavior Monitoring / Deceptive Alignment / Self-Improvement Risk & Control — a fresh capability-to-attack technique against persistent agent memory.
risks.derived_from: 2607.15657 (Lucid black-box adversarial framework compromising multimodal memory via unconditional trust in visual data); SOCIETAL_CONTEXT Security: June 2026 OpenAI disclosure of PRC-linked influence ops using ChatGPT to amplify the US data-center/electricity-cost debate; agentic deployment shifting risk toward long-horizon multi-step behavior outpacing monitoring.
risks.communities: Adversarial-ML / agent-memory-security researchers see that persistent multimodal memory can be poisoned via crafted visual inputs (they built Lucid); state-linked influence-operations analysts (OpenAI threat-intel, election-security teams) see that adversaries are already probing narratives around AI infrastructure. Neither connects the two: memory-security work treats poisoning as a technical integrity bug, and influence-ops analysts watch content generation, not the durable memory state of agents that will increasingly mediate information.
risks.framing_inversion: Conventional influence-ops framing: adversaries push malicious content that models might repeat. Inversion: as agents gain persistent memory, the durable target is the agent's memory state itself — a single poisoned visual episode can bias an agent's outputs long-term across many downstream users, shifting influence ops from high-volume content spraying to low-volume, persistent memory implantation.
risks.why_underdiscussed: Persistent-memory agents are new; the Lucid attack is this week; the documented state-linked influence op is recent and rated low-impact, so its convergence with memory-poisoning is not yet in threat models. Both halves exist but in separate briefings.
risks.mechanism: Multimodal agents increasingly trust visual data written to long-term memory (2607.15657) → an adversary plants crafted visual episodes that persist and bias future retrieval/generation → because agents will mediate search, summarization, and decision support at scale, a poisoned memory produces durable, hard-to-detect narrative bias → this is a natural upgrade path for the influence actors already testing AI-infrastructure narratives, on a 0–24 month horizon as memory-enabled agents deploy.
risks.leading_indicator: First disclosed incident (in a lab threat report or CISA/vendor advisory) of a production agent's long-term memory being poisoned via injected content, OR red-team demonstration of persistent narrative bias in a deployed retrieval-augmented agent traced to memory contamination.
risks.calibration: Emerging, medium confidence. The attack technique is observed (this week) and the influence-op probing is observed (societal context). The convergence — influence actors adopting memory-poisoning against deployed agents — is speculative and depends on memory-enabled agents reaching wide deployment. Enablement inference, not proof of abuse; confidence lowered.
risks.extrapolation: Assumed persistent-memory multimodal agents reach broad deployment within the window and that influence actors will move from content generation to memory targeting; the digest shows the attack technique and the influence-op interest separately, not their union.
risks.score_rationale: Severity 4 because durable, stealthy narrative manipulation of decision-mediating agents is systemic; likelihood 3 because both halves are demonstrated but their convergence depends on memory-agent deployment maturing; exposure 4 because poisoned memory in a widely-used agent affects many downstream users.

change_of_mind.upgrade_if: A threat-intel report documents memory-poisoning against a deployed agent, or influence-op tradecraft explicitly shifts toward persistent-memory targets.
change_of_mind.downgrade_if: Deployed memory-agents adopt provenance/integrity checks on stored episodes (signed memory, retrieval-time verification) that neutralize black-box visual poisoning.
change_of_mind.invalidate_if: Persistent-memory multimodal agents fail to reach meaningful deployment, or the Lucid-class attack proves non-transferable to production memory architectures with standard hardening.

action_map.eval_to_run: Red-team a production-style retrieval-augmented multimodal agent with Lucid-style visual memory injections and measure durability of narrative bias across sessions and users.
action_map.benchmark_to_monitor: Agentic Behavior Monitoring paper velocity vs. agent-deployment pace, and any emerging memory-integrity benchmark for persistent-memory agents.
action_map.mitigation: Require provenance/integrity verification on writes to long-term memory and retrieval-time trust scoring; quarantine memory written from untrusted visual sources.
action_map.policy_question: Should persistent-memory agents deployed at scale be treated as influence-op attack surfaces requiring threat-intel disclosure of memory-integrity incidents?
action_map.owner_community: Adversarial-ML/agent-security teams jointly with lab influence-operations threat-intelligence units.
action_map.data_source_to_watch: OpenAI/Anthropic/Google threat-intel reports, CISA advisories, and arXiv agent-memory-security papers building on 2607.15657.

claims.text: A this-week paper demonstrates a black-box adversarial framework poisoning multimodal agents' long-term memory via untrusted visual data.
PRC-linked influence operations using ChatGPT to amplify the US data-center/electricity debate were disclosed in June 2026.
Agentic deployment is shifting risk toward long-horizon, multi-step behavior outpacing existing monitoring.
Persistent memory poisoning could become a durable, stealthy influence-op vector as memory-enabled agents deploy.
Influence actors will adopt memory-poisoning tradecraft within 0-24 months.

verification.prior_coverage: Both halves of this risk are already public and prominent. Agent memory poisoning — including multimodal/visual 'sleeper' triggers that persist in long-term memory and bias future agent behavior — is a heavily published 2026 topic (multiple arXiv papers plus security-vendor writeups), and 'Visual Inception' (arXiv 2604.16966) already demonstrates persistent multimodal memory poisoning to steer long-horizon agentic recommender behavior. The PRC-linked ChatGPT influence op targeting the US data-center/electricity debate was disclosed by OpenAI and widely reported (Axios, Al Jazeera, CyberScoop, Tom's Hardware). What is NOT yet explicitly written up as a named seam is the specific convergence: influence actors upgrading from prompt-based amplification to persistent memory-poisoning of deployed multimodal agents.

verification.disputed_claims: Two dependencies are contested. (1) The influence-op attribution/impact: OpenAI itself labels the PRC operation only 'likely,' and its investigator frames it as an operation jumping onto a pre-existing genuine domestic debate; broader research (International AI Safety Report 2026; MIT Technology Review) holds that real-world AI-driven influence is documented but not yet widespread and that AI influence impact is frequently overblown. So 'PRC-linked influence op is meaningfully shifting the debate' is not firmly established. (2) The feasibility/scale of memory poisoning at influence scale is questioned by security practitioners (Govtech) who note poisoning enough controlled datasets to reliably shift outcomes is hard. The core memory-poisoning attack technique itself is not disputed — it is well demonstrated in lab settings — but its real-world exploitation against deployed agents is so far theoretical.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Lower to weak/speculative. Both components are already widely discussed, and the technical seam is close to being explicitly published: 'Visual Inception' (arXiv 2604.16966) already frames multimodal memory poisoning as persistent 'sleeper agent' triggers that hijack long-horizon agent behavior — so the memory-poisoning-as-durable-narrative-bias idea is not unsurfaced. The only genuinely fresh element is naming the explicit adoption path by known influence actors, and that path rests on two contested claims: the PRC op's real-world impact is only 'likely'-attributed and its persuasive effect is disputed/possibly overblown, and influence-scale memory poisoning of deployed agents remains unobserved and is doubted by practitioners on feasibility grounds. This is an enablement inference stacked on a contested-impact premise, not evidence of abuse. Brief it as a plausible watch-item convergence, not a confident emerging risk, and explicitly attach the caveats that (a) the influence op's efficacy is unproven and (b) memory poisoning has no documented real-world influence-campaign use.

risks.risk: Watermark mandates being written into law (SB 942, EU AI Act) rest on a forensic-reliability assumption that this week's empirical evidence shows is false — freezing an unsound evidentiary standard into enforcement just as classified frontier evals stop producing public counter-evidence.
risks.research_anchor: This week's paper 'AI Watermark Evidence Fails Forensic Readiness: An Empirical Evaluation' (2607.16010) directly tests and falsifies the 'sufficiently reliable and robust' assumption underlying EU AI Act and California SB 942 watermarking mandates.
risks.derived_from: 2607.16010 explicitly names EU AI Act and SB 942's 'permanent or extraordinarily difficult to remove' language as resting on an untested assumption; crossed with LIVE_WEB_BRIEF: SB 942 disclosure/watermarking effective Aug 2, 2026, and June 2 2026 EO moving CAISI evals to a classified framework and directing CAISI to stop publishing findings.
risks.communities: Watermark/provenance ML researchers see the technical fragility (they wrote 2607.16010); legislators and courts drafting/applying SB 942 and EU AI Act treat watermark presence/absence as reliable disclosure evidence. They are not connecting because the legal standard was set before the empirical forensic-readiness test existed, and the researchers publish to arXiv, not into rulemaking dockets or evidentiary case law.
risks.framing_inversion: Conventional framing: 'watermarking mandates are a solution to synthetic-media harm.' Inversion: a watermarking mandate can manufacture false evidentiary confidence — absence of a (removable) watermark becomes de facto proof of 'human-made,' and presence becomes spoofable evidence usable to frame or exonerate, degrading rather than improving forensic truth.
risks.why_underdiscussed: The mandates and the falsifying empirical paper are both brand new (law effective Aug 2026; paper this week), and the June 2 EO that would normally surface government counter-evidence just moved evals behind a classified wall — so the reliability gap has no public venue to appear in.
risks.mechanism: Law requires 'permanent/robust' watermarks → vendors ship watermarks that 2607.16010 shows fail forensic readiness → courts, platforms, and regulators begin treating watermark status as evidence of provenance → adversaries strip watermarks (to launder AI content as human) or forge them (to discredit genuine human content), while the classified-eval regime removes the public benchmark that could expose the failure.
risks.leading_indicator: First court filing, platform enforcement action, or regulatory penalty under SB 942 that relies on watermark presence/absence as dispositive evidence — especially any case where a watermark was later shown removable or spoofable.
risks.calibration: Emerging, medium confidence. The technical failure is observed (this week's empirical paper). The legal-reliance harm is inferred: it depends on courts/regulators actually treating watermarks as dispositive, which is not yet documented. Confidence lowered because the enforcement-behavior link is projected, not observed.
risks.extrapolation: Assumed that legal/enforcement actors will treat watermark status as strong evidence and that the classified-eval shift reduces public counter-evidence; neither is shown in the digest.
risks.score_rationale: Severity 3 because misattributed provenance can wrongly implicate/exonerate but is bounded; likelihood 3 because the law takes effect Aug 2026 and the technical failure is empirically shown, but courtroom reliance is unproven; exposure 4 because watermarking touches all AI-content disclosure across platforms and jurisdictions.

change_of_mind.upgrade_if: A documented legal or platform-enforcement decision hinges on watermark status AND an adversary is shown to have stripped/forged the mark in that context.
change_of_mind.downgrade_if: Regulators issue guidance explicitly stating watermark absence is not evidence of human authorship, or robust tamper-evident schemes pass independent forensic-readiness evaluation.
change_of_mind.invalidate_if: Follow-up evaluations show deployed watermarking schemes are in fact forensically robust to removal and forgery under adversarial conditions.

action_map.eval_to_run: Adversarial forensic-readiness test of every watermarking scheme that vendors intend to use for SB 942 compliance, replicating 2607.16010's removal/forgery attacks.
action_map.benchmark_to_monitor: Watermark survival rate under paraphrase/re-encode/crop attacks and forgery success rate, tracked over time as schemes update.
action_map.mitigation: Regulators publish an evidentiary caveat that watermark status is corroborative, not dispositive; require chain-of-custody metadata rather than watermark-only proof.
action_map.policy_question: Should statutory 'reliable and robust' language specify a measurable forensic-readiness threshold, and who certifies it once government evals are classified?
action_map.owner_community: Provenance/watermarking researchers + digital-forensics and evidence-law scholars jointly, with a state AG technical advisory role.

claims.text: A this-week empirical paper shows current watermark evidence fails forensic-readiness against the reliability standard EU AI Act and SB 942 codify.
SB 942 watermarking disclosure obligations take effect Aug 2, 2026.
The June 2, 2026 EO moved CAISI evaluations to a classified framework and halted public evaluation findings.
Legal and platform actors will increasingly treat watermark status as evidentiary, creating spoof/strip attack incentives.
Removing public government evals leaves no venue to surface deployed-watermark reliability gaps.

verification.prior_coverage: Nearly every component of this seam is already public. The technical claim (watermarks fail forensic/robustness readiness and can be removed or forged) is extensively documented across 2024-2026 research and press (Feizi/UMD 'broke all of them', VideoMarkBench, Warfare, diffusion-regeneration attacks). The 'watermark mandates rest on an untested/flawed technical assumption' thesis is the explicit argument of a 2025 position paper ('Watermarking Without Standards Is Not AI Governance'). And the conversion of provenance/watermark signals into contested legal evidence under SB 942 and EU AI Act Article 50 is already being written up by law firms and legal-tech commentators. The one comparatively fresh element is the specific stitch to the June 2 2026 EO classifying CAISI evaluations as removing the public counter-benchmark that would expose watermark failure.

verification.disputed_claims: Two contested load-bearing claims. (1) 'Empirical evidence shows watermark forensic reliability is false' is only partly settled: attack researchers (Feizi/UMD, VideoMarkBench, Warfare, diffusion-regeneration papers) show removal and forgery, but vendors/defenders dispute the framing — Google DeepMind's SynthID papers claim graded robustness against realistic threat models, and Hany Farid, while agreeing watermarking is attackable, still argues robust watermarking is 'part of the solution,' not worthless. So 'this week's paper shows it is false' overstates a genuinely contested finding rather than a closed one. (2) The characterization that courts/regulators already treat watermark status as dispositive provenance evidence is NOT documented — even the risk's own calibration concedes this is projected, and the legal-tech sources describe watermarks as 'contested facts' being fought over, not as accepted dispositive proof. The CAISI classification is real and reported (Crypto Briefing; Sen. Budd's pushback confirms it), but no source links it to watermark benchmarking specifically.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Downgrade from 'Emerging, medium confidence' to 'Largely anticipated, low-to-medium confidence.' The three main pillars are each already in public discourse: watermark forensic fragility (well-documented but contested, not a settled 'false' verdict), the claim that watermark mandates rest on an untested technical assumption (already the thesis of arXiv 2505.23814, which names the EU AI Act and US frameworks), and the conversion of provenance signals into contested legal evidence under SB 942/Article 50 (already covered by law firms and legal-tech writers). The risk should NOT be briefed as novel. Two confidence-lowering caveats must travel with it: (a) the 'empirical evidence shows reliability is false' framing launders a genuinely disputed research area into a settled fact — vendors (SynthID) and even skeptics like Farid contest the strong version; and (b) the core harm — courts/regulators treating watermark status as dispositive — remains inferred, not observed. The only defensibly fresh contribution is the narrow, still-speculative stitch that the June 2 2026 EO classifying CAISI evals removes the public benchmark that could expose watermark failure; that specific linkage is not yet in the literature and could be surfaced as a hypothesis, but only clearly labeled as unverified and dependent on the two contested claims above.

foresight_gap.context:
# Societal Context — current real-world state
#
# ILLUSTRATIVE AND NON-EXHAUSTIVE. This file enriches the foresight synthesis with
# CURRENT real-world state across societal domains. It is NOT a filter, a fixed list,
# or a set of "the risks that matter." The synthesis reasons across the FULL
# STEEP/PESTLE+ framework regardless of what is written here. The purpose of this file
# is solely to UPDATE the model on current real-world conditions it cannot otherwise
# know from training data — not to narrow, rank, or pre-select what it considers.
# The model must treat everything below as background state to reason ACROSS and
# combine with the week's research signals, never as the only factors in play.
#
# Write BROAD, FACTUAL, and CURRENT. State conditions of the world, not conclusions
# about which are dangerous. Give the board, not the move. Update regularly.
#
# Last updated: June 2026

## Social
- US public sentiment toward AI has soured over the past year. A March 2026 Quinnipiac poll found 55% of Americans think AI will do more harm than good in daily life (up from 44% in April 2025); 64% think AI will do more harm than good for education.
- A March 2026 NBC News poll found 57% of voters believe AI's risks outweigh its benefits (vs. 34% the reverse); only 26% have positive feelings about AI, 46% negative — a net rating lower than every tested topic except the Democratic Party and Iran.
- Trust is low and usage is rising simultaneously: a Feb 2026 Verasight poll found 56% remain anxious about AI even as adoption grows. The Stanford 2026 AI Index notes global "more benefits than drawbacks" sentiment rose to 59%, but nervousness also rose to 52%.
- Sentiment is sharply split by demographics (see Demographic). The single strongest predictor of attitude is personal usage: daily users are favorable by +57 points; rare/never users unfavorable by -42.
- 76% of Americans think businesses are not transparent enough about their AI use; trust in AI developers is a recurring weak point.

## Technological
- Frontier capability is advancing fastest in agentic/autonomous systems and coding. Labs report models authoring large shares of their own code; autonomous task-completion horizons are lengthening.
- Capability is increasingly announced first by labs (system cards, blog posts) ahead of the academic safety literature's response — the lead-time gap this tool measures.
- Synthetic media generation is mainstream (a quarter of 2026 Super Bowl ads were AI-made or AI-advertising); detection and provenance lag generation.
- Agentic deployment is shifting risk from single-output failures toward long-horizon, multi-step, multi-tool behavior — outpacing existing monitoring designed for bounded tasks.

## Economic
- Intense "AI bubble" debate. Total AI spending projected to surpass $1.6T; OpenAI committed ~$1.4T over 8 years on data centers; the big four (Google, Microsoft, Meta, Amazon) expected to spend ~$450B in capex in 2026; the 14 largest data-center operators near $750B in 2026 (up from ~$450B in 2025); Goldman estimates ~$7.6T cumulative AI CapEx 2026–2031.
- Concentration risk: in late 2025 the five largest companies held up ~30% of the S&P 500 — the greatest concentration in half a century. AI-related stocks have driven ~75% of S&P 500 returns, 80% of earnings growth, and 90% of capex growth since ChatGPT launched.
- Circular-investment concerns (Nvidia↔OpenAI↔AMD↔Microsoft↔CoreWeave cross-holdings) fuel bubble skepticism. Hyperscalers took on ~$121B in new debt (+300% vs. typical load), some structured off-balance-sheet via special-purpose vehicles.
- Revenue/ROI doubts: an MIT study found 95% of organizations saw zero return on GenAI investment; one analysis found only ~3% of people pay for AI. A Feb 2026 NBER study found 90% of firms reported no productivity/workplace impact.
- Institutional warnings: the Bank of England and IMF have warned of global market-correction risk from AI overvaluation. Counter-view (JPMorgan, Fed Chair Powell, White House AI czar David Sacks, Ben Horowitz) holds AI generates real revenue and is not a classic bubble. DeepSeek's Jan 2025 release triggered a ~$600B single-day Nvidia drop; the KOSPI halted trading on June 23, 2026.

## Environmental
- Energy is becoming a binding constraint on AI buildout. The IEA projects global data-center electricity use rising from ~415 TWh in 2024 (~1.5% of total) toward ~945 TWh by 2030 (roughly 4×).
- Data-center siting, water use, and grid strain are emerging as local political flashpoints. Public opinion favors data-center approaches that protect consumers from high electricity costs and environmental impact.
- Grid capacity, power procurement, and cooling are now first-order bottlenecks alongside chip supply.

## Political
- The federal posture is deregulatory and pro-preemption. A Dec 11, 2025 executive order ("Ensuring a National Policy Framework for AI," EO 14365) directs a DOJ litigation task force (active Jan 10, 2026) to challenge state AI laws, a Commerce review of "burdensome" state laws, and an FTC policy stance; it exempts child safety, AI compute/data-center infrastructure, and state procurement from preemption.
- In March 2026 the White House released a National Policy Framework urging Congress to enact broad preemption under a light-touch standard.
- AI is politically "up for grabs" — voters trust neither party to handle it well; negative sentiment concentrates among younger voters and women under 50.
- A notable administration reversal: driven by national-security concerns over advanced model capabilities, the administration is reportedly considering pre-release frontier-model evaluation requirements, and CAISI (the renamed US AI Safety Institute) announced evaluation partnerships with Google, Microsoft, and xAI — a shift for an administration that entered opposing AI oversight.

## Legal / Regulatory
- Preemption is NOT settled law. A 10-year state-law moratorium failed in the Senate 99-1 (July 2025). State laws remain enforceable unless/until preempted by statute or struck down by courts. Prudent posture is to keep complying with state law while monitoring.
- California SB 53 (Transparency in Frontier AI Act) is in effect as of Jan 1, 2026: frontier developers (>10^26 FLOPS) must publish risk frameworks, report safety incidents, and provide whistleblower protections; penalties up to $1M/violation for firms over $500M revenue. Also effective: AB 2013 (training-data transparency); SB 942 (AI-content disclosure/watermarking, delayed to Aug 2, 2026).
- Texas TRAIGA (HB 149) in force Jan 1, 2026 — scaled back from an EU-style draft to prohibiting specific harmful practices, intent-based liability, AG enforcement with a 60-day cure period.
- Colorado's comprehensive SB 24-205 never took effect; in May 2026 it was repealed/replaced by the narrower SB 26-189 (ADMT-focused), effective Jan 1, 2027. It was the only state law named in the Dec 2025 EO.
- New York amended the RAISE Act (March 2026) to mirror SB 53 (revenue-based threshold), easing multistate compliance.
- A bipartisan federal discussion draft — the Great American AI Act (June 4, 2026, Obernolte/Trahan, 269 pages) — proposes the first comprehensive federal framework in exchange for a 3-year freeze on state laws regulating how models are *built* (not how they're *used*). Not yet introduced or voted on.
- State legislative volume is high: ~1,561 AI bills introduced across 45 states as of March 2026, surpassing all of 2024.
- EU AI Act: GPAI obligations took effect Aug 2, 2025; high-risk obligations have an Aug 2, 2026 deadline, though the Commission proposed (Nov 2025) delaying high-risk implementation toward 2027. It remains the binding global benchmark.

## Security / Geopolitical
- State-linked misuse of frontier AI is documented and rising. In June 2026, OpenAI disclosed PRC-linked influence operations using ChatGPT to amplify US domestic grievances — notably targeting the data-center/electricity-cost debate — rated low-impact (Category One) but notable for testing narratives against AI infrastructure itself.
- US–China competition centers on compute: export controls on advanced chips and high-bandwidth memory; Chinese stockpiling ahead of restrictions; Chinese model developers (DeepSeek and others) demonstrating competitive capability at lower cost.
- Frontier cyber capability is operationalizing: documented cases of AI-assisted/AI-orchestrated cyber operations and the first AI-developed exploit code; labs now rate some models "High" in cyber capability.
- National-security concern over advanced model capabilities is reshaping US oversight posture (see Political) — government model-review arrangements with major labs are now in place.
- Critical-infrastructure exposure (power grid, financial systems) to AI-enabled attacks is a growing institutional concern.

## Demographic
- Attitudes split sharply by group. Data for Progress (Feb 2026): men favorable +16, women unfavorable -10; under-45 favorable +25, 45+ unfavorable -10; Black voters +29 and Latino +10 favorable, white voters -3.
- Generational pessimism is strongest among the young despite high usage: Quinnipiac found Gen Z 36% support / 58% oppose AI, the most negative of any generation; NBC found 18-34s at net -44 favorability and women 18-49 at -41.
- A usage/education divergence at work: among employed college-educated voters, daily AI use jumped from 22% (Aug 2025) to 34% (early 2026), while non-college use fell ~6 points — a widening adoption gap.
- Overall adoption keeps rising: ~56-66% report using an AI tool in recent months; professional/managerial and white-collar workers adopt far faster than blue-collar workers and retirees.

# Current-State Brief — as of late June 2026
**Flagged topics: Reward Hacking, Scalable Oversight**

**June 2, 2026 — US government evaluation posture reversed (updates standing context).** President Trump signed an executive order on June 2, 2026 that shifts control of AI model assessments from CAISI to a classified framework run by national security agencies, and the order mandates a 30-day government review of AI models before companies can release them publicly. This significantly updates the standing context, which described CAISI's lab partnerships as an open oversight expansion; CAISI has now been directed to stop publishing evaluation findings.

**May 5, 2026 — CAISI evaluation partnerships (context confirmed, then curtailed).** On May 5, 2026, CAISI announced pre-deployment testing agreements with Google DeepMind, Microsoft, and xAI, expanding its frontier model evaluation program to five major labs alongside existing partners OpenAI and Anthropic. These pre-date the June 2 reclassification above.

**May 7, 2026 — EU AI Act high-risk deadline slipped (updates Aug 2, 2026 date).** High-risk obligations for stand-alone Annex III systems are deferred to 2 December 2027; for AI embedded in regulated products under Annex I, to 2 August 2028.

**May 14, 2026 — Colorado.** Colorado Governor Polis signed SB 26-189, which repeals and reenacts the Colorado AI Act, effective January 1, 2027.

**July 6, 2026 — Illinois (new, relevant to oversight/audits).** Illinois Governor Pritzker signed SB 315, the AI Safety Measures Act, establishing the first mandatory annual third-party audit requirement for large frontier developers; the law takes effect Jan. 1, 2027.

**June 4, 2026 — Federal preemption still only a draft.** The Great American AI Act, released June 4, 2026, would preempt certain state AI-development laws for three years but would leave much of the current state patchwork intact. Still a discussion draft, not introduced or voted.
